title: English Conquest of Ireland
summary: The checked-in EngConIre XML is a repository copy of the University of Michigan's 1999 encoding of the 1969 Greenwood reprint of Frederick J. Furnivall's 1896 EETS parallel-text edition. Furnivall printed the complete Middle English work in two witness versions: Trinity College Dublin MS 592, formerly E.2.31, and Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. B. 490. The Rawlinson path runs through a working transcript made by Mrs Parker and proofs collated against the manuscript by George Parker; the edition also prints a narrowly scoped specimen from TCD MS 593, formerly F.4.4, and consults James F. Dimock's 1867 Latin edition for source comparison and notes. The abstract Middle English work is an anonymous, freely abridged and rearranged Hiberno-English version of Gerald of Wales's Expugnatio Hibernica; its exact Latin exemplar is unidentified. A complete scan of the 1896 edition is cached with OCR and provider metadata. TCD's records describe digitized versions of MSS 592 and 593, but automated image or IIIF retrieval was blocked by an interactive CAPTCHA, so the cached DOI records are metadata only and no TCD facsimile is asserted. No public Digital Bodleian object for Rawl. B. 490 was found in a dated API search; reading-room and paid complete-object copying remain request routes.
review_notes: The current TCD crosswalk is E.2.31 = IE TCD MS 592 and F.4.4 = IE TCD MS 593. MS 593 contributes only Furnivall's pp. xiv-xvi specimen and is not a source for either complete body stream.
The XML HEADER's AUTHOR value for Giraldus Cambrensis is inherited edition-level cataloging. It does not make Gerald the author of the anonymous Middle English wording.
The CME submodule revision is e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c. The exact EngConIre.xml git blob is 31c3b76dd2c44ae2a6f881446c8da8bf7d8966b4; these two identifiers are not interchangeable.
The present Bodleian catalog dates MS. Rawl. B. 490 to 1400-1410, whereas Furnivall dated it about 1440. Both statements are retained at their own evidence layers.
No complete manuscript-image bundle is present in source-cache/EngConIre. The TCD JSON files and Bodleian TEI file are metadata-only research copies.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for EngConIre
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item EngConIre
entities.description: A 1999 manually keyed and reviewed encoding of the 1969 Greenwood reprint. It preserves Furnivall's front matter, two complete witness texts, notes, printed-page breaks, and manuscript-leaf milestones, while linearizing parallel print and excluding the glossary and indexes.
notes: The header says the text was keyboarded to an error-rate target of no more than one error per 20,000 characters, then 5 percent was reviewed before Kenneth Church marked it up and Catherine E. Paul reviewed the markup.

The current U-M collection page states No Copyright-United States, while the legacy XML header preserves an older reuse clause that permits individual personal, research, and teaching use but forbids redistribution by content providers.

entities.label: Anonymous Middle English English Conquest of Ireland
entities.description: The abstract Hiberno-Middle English prose translation, abridgment, and adaptation represented by multiple manuscript copies. It is one work across divergent witnesses, not Gerald of Wales's Latin work, a manuscript object, or Furnivall's two-column edition.

date_statements.label: Secure first attestation from a currently dated source witness
date_statements.value: By 1400-1410, the present catalog date of MS. Rawl. B. 490
date_statements.notes: This is a terminus from a witness date, not an exact translation date.

notes: Modern scholarship treats the lost original translation as a Hiberno-English version made in Ireland for a colonial English audience and surviving in several fifteenth- and sixteenth-century copies.

The translation is shorter than the Latin work, reorders and omits material, enlarges selected passages, and appears to depend on an intermediate recension; no physical Latin exemplar has been identified.

entities.label: Gerald of Wales, Expugnatio Hibernica
entities.description: Gerald of Wales's Latin history of the Anglo-Norman invasion and conquest of Ireland. This abstract source-work entity does not identify the particular Latin manuscript or recension used by the anonymous Middle English translator.

entities.label: Greenwood Press reprint of The English Conquest of Ireland (1969)
entities.description: The immediate print source named in the U-M XML header: a 1969 EETS Old Series 107 reprint of Furnivall's 1896 edition.

bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., The English Conquest of Ireland, A.D. 1166-1185, EETS Original Series 107 (New York: Greenwood Press, 1969), reprint of the 1896 edition.
bibliographic.title: The English Conquest of Ireland, A.D. 1166-1185
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 107

entities.label: Frederick J. Furnivall, The English Conquest of Ireland (1896)
alternate_labels: The English Conquest of Ireland, A.D. 1166-1185
EETS Original Series 107, Part I: The Text
entities.description: Furnivall's parallel-text edition of the complete Middle English work in TCD MS 592 and Bodleian MS. Rawl. B. 490, with forewords, extensive Latin comparison notes, a specimen from TCD MS 593, a note on untranslated final Latin chapters, a glossary, and indexes. The U-M encoding omits the glossary and indexes and does not reproduce the Henry Bradley correction leaf visible in the cached 1896 copy.

bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., The English Conquest of Ireland, A.D. 1166-1185: Mainly from the Expugnatio Hibernica of Giraldus Cambrensis, Part I, The Text, Early English Text Society, Original Series 107 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1896).
bibliographic.title: The English Conquest of Ireland, A.D. 1166-1185
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 107
bibliographic.pages: xvi + 172 printed pages, with an unnumbered correction leaf

physical_description.extent: Preliminaries through p. xvi; main parallel text and back note pp. 1-152; glossary and indexes pp. 153-172; unnumbered Henry Bradley correction leaf

notes: Mrs Parker copied MS. Rawl. B. 490; George Parker collated its proofs against the manuscript; Thomas Austin prepared the glossary and indexes; John Kells Ingram supplied English side-notes to even pp. 10-32, with two or three altered by Furnivall.
Furnivall had TCD MS 592 in hand and read proofs against it. The accessible forewords do not name a separate TCD working-copy maker.

Furnivall says Dimock's notes, dates, and preface were of the greatest use; the many Latin parallels and source discussions therefore belong to the editorial apparatus, not to the Middle English work's language facet.

entities.label: Mrs Parker's working transcript of MS. Rawl. B. 490
entities.description: The material transcript commissioned by Furnivall from Mrs Parker and used to set the Rawlinson column. It is provisionally typed catalog_description because schema 1.0.0 has no dedicated working-transcript entity type; it is not a medieval witness.

entities.label: James F. Dimock, Giraldi Cambrensis Opera, volume V (1867)
entities.description: The Rolls Series Latin edition of the Expugnatio Hibernica consulted by Furnivall for notes, dates, prefatory source analysis, and Latin comparison passages. It is a scholarly intermediary for the apparatus, not the anonymous translator's proven physical exemplar.

bibliographic.citation: James F. Dimock, ed., Giraldi Cambrensis Opera, vol. V, Rerum Britannicarum Medii Aevi Scriptores 21 (London: Longmans, Green, Reader, and Dyer, 1867).
bibliographic.title: Giraldi Cambrensis Opera, volume V

entities.label: Dublin, Trinity College Library, IE TCD MS 592
entities.description: The principal Dublin witness printed in Furnivall's even-page column. The English Conquest occupies fols. 1r-27v; fol. 28r contains an unrelated fragment of the dedication to James Yonge's Gouvernaunce of Princes and an incomplete English prayer. Those adjacent items are outside the EngConIre work boundary.

date_statements.label: Furnivall's witness date
date_statements.notes: The accessible current DOI metadata does not supply a normalized manuscript date, so Furnivall's historical dating remains explicitly qualified.

holding.notes: The institutional DOI record links the present catalog entry and documents the old edition context.

physical_description.extent: ii + fols. 1-28 + ii; English Conquest on fols. 1r-27v
notes: The DOI description records red and green decoration, marginal sketches, later material on fol. 28r, and a tooled leather binding.
No support material is asserted here because the accessible DOI metadata did not state it.

The current TCD prose description calls the work 'by Thomas Bray' and includes James Yonge in creator metadata, but the same record locates Yonge's distinct text only on fol. 28r. Neither name is treated here as confirmed author of the Conquest.
The XML's final Dublin milestone values '42b.' and '7b.' conflict with the otherwise sequential foliation and the current official locus ending at 27v.

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Rawl. B. 490
entities.description: A parchment codex containing the English Conquest of Ireland followed by the Middle English Secreta secretorum translated by James Yonge. The Conquest is represented by Furnivall and the XML from fol. 1r through fol. 28r; the current abbreviated catalog does not itself state that locus.

date_statements.label: Furnivall's witness date
date_statements.notes: Retained as historical edition metadata; it conflicts with the present catalog's 1400-1410 range.

notes: The current summary record does not provide a complete foliation, dimensions, collation, binding description, or item-level loci.
Richard Rawlinson owned the codex and bequeathed it to the Bodleian in 1755.

entities.label: Dublin, Trinity College Library, IE TCD MS 593
entities.description: A paper manuscript whose pp. 1-55 contain a later English copy of the Conquest and whose pp. 58-182 contain extracts and notes from other chronicles in multiple sixteenth- and seventeenth-century hands. EngConIre includes only Furnivall's specimen from the middle of MS 593 p. 7, printed on edition pp. xiv-xvi for comparison with chapter XIII.

date_statements.label: Manuscript production
date_statements.notes: The Conquest section is in a Tudor secretary hand; later sections contain several additional hands.

physical_description.extent: pp. 1-182; Conquest on pp. 1-55; Furnivall specimen begins at p. 7, middle
notes: The DOI description says the tightly bound manuscript cannot be collated without distress and records a single vase-and-flower watermark.

Furnivall says this was reported to be a sixteenth-century transcript of E.2.31/MS 592. The currently accessible institutional metadata calls MS 592 an earlier version but does not prove direct copying, so no direct witness-to-witness relation is asserted.

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Furnivall's 1896 edition
entities.description: A 198-page text PDF derived from a 500-ppi University of Toronto/Pratt scan of the complete 1896 book, accompanied by OCR and item metadata. It is a print-edition facsimile, not a manuscript facsimile.
entities.reproduction_format: Complete 198-page text PDF plus OCR derivatives

agents.name: Kenneth Church
notes: Credited in the XML header with marking up the electronic text.

agents.name: Catherine E. Paul
notes: Credited in the XML header with a 5 percent text review and markup review.

agents.name: Mrs Parker
notes: Furnivall identifies her only as Mrs Parker and credits her with copying MS. Rawl. B. 490. No fuller identity was established in the inspected sources.

agents.name: George Parker
notes: Furnivall credits him with collating the Rawlinson proofs against the manuscript.

agents.name: Thomas Austin
notes: Furnivall credits him with the glossary and indexes to both witness texts.

agents.name: John Kells Ingram
notes: Initially intended to edit the TCD manuscript and later supplied English side-notes to even pp. 10-32.

agents.name: Henry Bradley
notes: Named on the correction leaf bound after the MS 593 specimen in the cached 1896 copy.

locators: fols. 1r-28r as represented by the edition

object.description: A passage corresponding to chapter XIII in the two complete witness streams

object.description: Gerald's Latin Expugnatio Hibernica as a source work

access.access_method: Read the current Western Medieval Manuscripts record or its pinned public TEI source.
access.format: HTML catalog and TEI XML source

local_copies.label: Pinned current Bodleian catalog TEI for MS. Rawl. B. 490

notes: The current catalog record has no digital-facsimile link. Digital Bodleian API searches for the exact shelfmark, expanded shelfmark, Summary Catalogue number, and work title returned no MS. Rawl. B. 490 object.
This dated negative finding does not prove that the manuscript has never been photographed or that no internal surrogate exists.

access.access_method: Register for Bodleian Archives & Manuscripts Requests and order the manuscript to the Weston Library Level 1 reading room, subject to item-specific review.
Request reference-quality PDF scans through Bodleian special-collections mediated copying; contact mediated.copying@bodleian.ox.ac.uk.
notes: For a preservation-complete request, ask first for the repository's verified total foliation and then request the whole physical object: covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, all front and rear endleaves including blanks, every recto and verso, and any inserted or folded surfaces. Map the corpus work separately to fols. 1r-28r after delivery.

The public mediated-copying page charges non-Oxford users £1 per scan and says legal or conservation restrictions can prevent scanning. Complete-object feasibility and price require an item-specific response.
Delivery does not itself establish permission for repository redistribution or commercial print-on-demand use.

rights.component: Underlying medieval Middle English wording and Gerald's twelfth-century source work
rights.basis: The source work and anonymous translation are medieval. This conclusion does not extend to modern transcriptions, editorial apparatus, catalog metadata, or manuscript photographs.

rights.component: 1896 edition, transcript, introduction, notes, glossary, indexes, and correction leaf
rights.basis: The book was published in 1896, well before the U.S. public-domain cutoff for published works, and U-M currently identifies the item and collection as public domain in the United States.

rights.component: 1969 reprint production and any reprint-specific matter
rights.basis: The underlying 1896 edition is public domain, but no inspected 1969 copy or item-specific notice establishes whether Greenwood added protectable matter or asserted separate rights in the reprint layer.

rights.component: University of Michigan CME digital text and markup
rights.basis: The current U-M item and collection pages state that the materials are in the public domain in the United States and apply the No Copyright-United States statement.
notes: The XML preserves a legacy availability statement permitting individual personal, research, teaching, and linking uses while forbidding redistribution by commercial or nonprofit content providers. The current public-domain statement appears broader, but the inspected pages do not explicitly explain whether the legacy clause was superseded; downstream users should preserve the conflict rather than silently choose one.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan derivatives and access layer
rights.basis: Internet Archive metadata records possible-copyright-status NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT for the United States, based on the 1896 publication date and absence of a visible notice. It supplies no separate open license for scan derivatives.

rights.component: TCD digital manuscript images and delivery layer
rights.basis: The accessible DataCite record has an empty rights list, and CAPTCHA-blocked image delivery prevented inspection of any manifest-level rights statement or image license.

rights.component: TCD digital manuscript images and delivery layer
rights.basis: The accessible DataCite record has an empty rights list, and CAPTCHA-blocked image delivery prevented inspection of any manifest-level rights statement or image license.

rights.component: Future reference scans supplied through Bodleian mediated copying
rights.basis: The current service page says delivered images are subject to Imaging Services terms of use. No item-specific delivery, license, or reuse permission has been received for MS. Rawl. B. 490.
Local caching records evidence and fixity; it does not establish permission to redistribute provider metadata, scan derivatives, or future manuscript photographs through Git or publication packages.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Two complete manuscript versions with omissions and variant wording
editorial_practices.representation: TCD MS 592 on even pages and MS. Rawl. B. 490 on facing odd pages
editorial_practices.description: Furnivall prints the two witness texts in parallel so that each can clarify the other's omissions; he does not silently merge them into one critical text.

editorial_practices.representation: Mostly editor-supplied chapter heads, bracketed supplies, and parenthetical text assumed to come from other manuscripts
editorial_practices.description: The XML header says Furnivall supplied all chapter heads except Dublin XXVII, XXIX, LI and Rawlinson XXVII, XXIX; all bracketed text is his, and U-M inferred that parenthetical text was also supplied from other manuscripts.
notes: The chapter count is consequently an edition/encoding structure and not securely intrinsic to the medieval work.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Corresponding and omitted material in Gerald's Latin work
editorial_practices.representation: Latin parallels, source references, side-notes, and a back note on untranslated chapters
editorial_practices.description: Furnivall uses Dimock's 1867 edition to compare the English text with several Latin recensions and to identify material not translated.
editorial_practices.scope: Forewords, notes throughout pp. 1-151, and back matter pp. 150-152

editorial_practices.representation: Front matter, witness texts, notes, and back note encoded; glossary and indexes excluded
editorial_practices.description: The XML header explicitly excludes the glossary and indexes. The cached 1896 scan also contains a Henry Bradley correction leaf that is not present in the XML, but its presence in the immediate 1969 source copy is unverified.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Facing parallel witness columns and parallel quotations in the print
editorial_practices.representation: Sequential DIV1 witness streams and sequentialized quotations, with some preliminary page numbers repeated
editorial_practices.description: The electronic item linearizes simultaneous print. EngConIre:3 is the complete TCD MS 592 stream and EngConIre:4 the complete Rawlinson stream; they are alternative versions of one work, not consecutive halves.
editorial_practices.scope: Forewords and both main witness divisions

editorial_practices.source_feature: Printed page breaks and manuscript folio markers, sometimes inside words or ambiguously placed
editorial_practices.representation: 171 PB elements and 112 MILESTONE elements; split words joined before markers; ambiguous milestones positioned by encoder judgment
editorial_practices.description: U-M documents moving joined words before page or folio breaks and choosing positions for unclear milestone markers. Two obvious late Dublin milestone values, 42b and 7b, remain inconsistent with the sequence and current 1r-27v locus.
editorial_practices.scope: Whole electronic item, especially the two complete witness streams

editorial_practices.source_feature: Long displaced preliminary note, omitted print note markers, paired note markers around English passages, and six macron-bearing ampersands
editorial_practices.representation: Preliminary note moved into p. vii note; two missing markers supplied; English quotation duplicated into long Latin notes; six ampersands normalized
editorial_practices.description: The XML header records these representation changes explicitly. They are digital editorial interventions and should not be projected back onto the manuscripts.
editorial_practices.scope: Selected notes and characters across the electronic item

evidence.citation: Kyvero-Press/CME, EngConIre.xml at submodule revision e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: HEADER, FRONT, BODY DIV1 nodes EngConIre:3-4, and BACK EngConIre:5
evidence.summary: Establishes the exact local artifact, U-M and source-edition statements, legacy availability clause, electronic production credits, detailed encoding practices, two complete witness streams, MS 593 specimen, and back note.

evidence.locator: whole XML tree and per-DIV1 element counts
evidence.summary: The 412,965-byte XML contains 5 DIV1, 127 DIV2, 130 HEAD, 171 PB, 112 MILESTONE, 196 NOTE1, 185 P, 43 CORR, and 146 SUPPLIED elements. EngConIre:3 and :4 each contain 62 chapter DIV2 nodes and 75 PB elements; the Dublin stream has 54 folio milestones and the Rawlinson stream 56. These are representation metrics, not counts of two separate works.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, English conquest of Ireland, item EngConIre.
evidence.locator: About this Item, publication, rights/permissions, and full-text forewords
evidence.summary: Identifies the item, Furnivall, Greenwood 1969, the source witnesses and collaborators, and the current public-domain rights statement.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., The English Conquest of Ireland, A.D. 1166-1185, EETS Original Series 107 (London, 1896), complete Internet Archive scan.
evidence.locator: title page; Forewords pp. vii-xiii; MS 593 specimen pp. xiv-xvi; unnumbered correction leaf; main text pp. 1-151; back note pp. 150-152; glossary and indexes pp. 153-172
evidence.summary: Provides the complete edition, primary statements for the manuscript-to-edition chains and collaborator roles, the exact MS 593 sample locus, Dimock dependence, print layout, paratext boundary, and matter omitted from the XML.

notes: The provider title has a transcription typo, 'about 425 A.D.', for the TCD witness. The scanned title page itself reads about 1425.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Special collections mediated copying.

evidence.summary: Offers reference-quality PDF scans to all requesters, currently at £1 per scan for non-Oxford users, subject to legal and conservation review and Imaging Services terms.

evidence.citation: Caoimhe Whelan, 'Translating the Expugnatio Hibernica: A Vernacular English History in Late Medieval Ireland,' in Text, Transmission, and Transformation in the European Middle Ages, 1000-1500, ed. Carrie Griffin and Emer Purcell (Turnhout: Brepols, 2018), 165-192, https://doi.org/10.1484/M.CURSOR-EB.5.114653.
evidence.summary: Treats the work as a Hiberno-Middle English translation and adaptation made in Ireland for a late-medieval colonial English audience, distinguishes the translation from Gerald's Latin work, and analyzes its omissions, additions, political framing, and transmission.

evidence.citation: Caoimhe Whelan, 'New Readers, Old History: Gerald of Wales and the Anglo-Norman Invasion of Ireland,' in Writing History in the Anglo-Norman World: Manuscripts, Makers and Readers, c.1066-c.1250, ed. Laura Cleaver and Andrea Worm (York: York Medieval Press, 2018), 239-255, https://doi.org/10.1515/9781787442894-015.
evidence.locator: pp. 239-255, especially the witness census and discussion of translation method and readership
evidence.summary: Identifies eight surviving English texts, says the lost original used an unidentified intermediate Latin recension, describes the stable historical core and Ireland-based readership, and explains abbreviation, reordering, omission, and selected expansion in the vernacular work.

evidence.citation: U.S. Copyright Office, Circular 15A, Duration of Copyright.
evidence.locator: published works and public-domain term tables
evidence.summary: Official term guidance supporting the United States public-domain analysis for an 1896 publication.

open_questions.question: Which Latin recension and physical exemplar underlie the lost original Middle English translation?
next_steps: Compare the complete English witness corpus against current Latin-recension scholarship rather than equating the source with Dimock's 1867 edition or Furnivall's Harley 177 comparison.
Consult Caoimhe Whelan's 2015 doctoral thesis and any later stemmatic work if an authorized copy becomes available.
notes: The relation to Expugnatio Hibernica is secure at work level; the physical source chain remains unknown.

open_questions.question: What evidence, if any, supports TCD's current 'by Thomas Bray' wording or any attribution of the Conquest to James Yonge?
next_steps: Ask TCD Manuscripts & Archives for the source and status of the Thomas Bray attribution in the MS 592 record.
Separate James Yonge's fol. 28r Gouvernaunce of Princes fragment and the Rawlinson Secreta from the anonymous Conquest in all future cataloging.
Review Whelan's thesis and current IPMEP authority data for explicit authorship discussion.

open_questions.question: Can the date and place of the lost archetypal Hiberno-English translation be narrowed beyond production in Ireland by the early fifteenth-century Rawlinson witness?
next_steps: Use all independent witnesses and current dialect/localization scholarship; do not transfer one copyist's dialect or Furnivall's conjectured fourteenth-century date to the archetype.
Review later work on the translation's audience, patronage, and manuscript stemma.

open_questions.question: Can complete, licensed image inventories or IIIF manifests for TCD MSS 592 and 593 be obtained without relying on an interactive CAPTCHA?
next_steps: Open each DOI in a normal interactive browser and inspect the network or download controls after human verification, without bypassing access controls.
Ask TCD Digital Collections for canonical manifests, canvas counts, original-file downloads, and item-level image rights statements.
If public payloads are supplied, cache and audit the complete physical object before extracting the EngConIre work portion.

open_questions.question: Does a complete internal or requestable digital surrogate of MS. Rawl. B. 490 already exist, and what is the codex's verified total foliation?
next_steps: Ask Bodleian Special Collections whether prior whole-object photography or microfilm exists and request its complete inventory.
If none exists, request a conservation and price assessment for full-object mediated copying or Imaging Services photography.
Confirm total leaves, endleaves, binding surfaces, and work locus before specifying a complete order.

open_questions.question: Is the Conquest in TCD MS 593 demonstrably a direct transcript of TCD MS 592, as reported by Furnivall, or only a later related version?
next_steps: Compare complete images or diplomatic transcriptions of MS 593 pp. 1-55 with MS 592 fols. 1r-27v, including shared errors and omissions.
Consult the full TCD catalog description and modern stemmatic scholarship before asserting a direct witness-to-witness relation.

open_questions.question: Should the Dublin milestone values 42b and final 7b be corrected to 24v and 27v, and did the 1969 source copy include Henry Bradley's correction leaf?
next_steps: Compare the exact 1969 reprint pages with the 1896 scan and, if possible, TCD MS 592 images before changing source XML.
Treat current XML values as representation data and the official 1r-27v locus as catalog evidence until a separate correction task is authorized.

open_questions.question: Does Mrs Parker's working transcript of MS. Rawl. B. 490 survive in Furnivall, EETS, Clarendon Press, or Parker papers?
next_steps: Search Furnivall and EETS archival finding aids for Rawlinson B. 490, Mrs Parker, and George Parker correspondence or proofs.
Do not infer the transcript's loss merely from its absence in the inspected edition and online catalogs.
